coredata.dc:title: Solution plasma synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from glucosamines: Comparative fluorescence modulation for dopamine detection

coredata.dc:description: The intriguing fluorescence characteristics of carbon dots (CDs) have led to many sensor applications, particularly for dopamine (DA), a molecule linked to various diseases. This study prepares CDs from glucose and glucosamine (nitrogen-free and nitrogen-containing precursors) using a simple solution plasma process (SPP). We explore how nitrogen elements and counterions in glucosamine (hydrochloride and sulfate) affect CD properties and their ability to modulate fluorescence for DA detection. Glucosamine offers three advantages over glucose: (i) single-step CD synthesis via SPP, (ii) enhanced fluorescence of CDs, and (iii) improved fluorescence response to DA. We investigate the DA detection capabilities of N,S-CDs (from glucosamine sulfate) and N,Cl-CDs (from glucosamine hydrochloride). Both can sense DA with distinct photoluminescence responses. N,S-CDs show selectivity and fluorescence brightening upon DA interaction, with a detection limit of 33.05 μM. N,Cl-CDs demonstrate higher sensitivity with a lower detection limit of 0.1212 μM through fluorescence quenching. Silver ions (Ag+) may also contribute to N,Cl-CDs quenching; however, the observed color change to gray due to silver nanoparticle (AgNP) formation helps pinpoint the quenching substance. The varied photoluminescence responses arise from different surface functional groups: N,S-CDs have amino-rich surfaces, while N,Cl-CDs have conjugated carbonyl-rich surfaces. This study illustrates the mechanisms of DA detection and AgNP formation, suggesting the potential of CDs in medical diagnostics as selective tools for disease diagnosis. Additionally, we compare the DA sensing methodology of our CDs with existing literature, highlighting the advantages of our sensor.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram depicting (a) the experimental setup of the solution plasma process (SPP) and (b) the chemical structures of glucose, glucosamine sulfate, and glucosamine hydrochloride, along with the observed phenomena after SPP treatment. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Photoluminescent characteristics of (a) pristine CDs, (b) N,S-CDs, and (c) N,Cl-CDs, including UV–visible absorption and fluorescence (FL) emission spectra. (d) Quantum yield percentages (QY%) of each CD type were determined at specific excitation wavelengths using a standard calibration method. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) TEM images and size distribution graphs, (b) XRD patterns, (c) Raman spectra with deconvolution of D-band and G-band, (d) FTIR spectra, and (e) XPS full survey scan spectra with percentage elemental composition, of pristine CDs, N,S-CDs, and N,Cl-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, S 2p, and Cl 2p for pristine CDs, N,S-CDs, and N,Cl-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Tauc plot analysis represents direct band gap energies of (a) pristine CDs, (b) N,S-CDs, and (c) N,Cl-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The relationship between chemical characteristics of CDs and their energy band gap.

(a) pH-dependent fluorescence (FL) characteristics of N,S-CDs and N,Cl-CDs. (b) Fluorescence intensity variations over different incubation times upon interaction with dopamine (DA). Fluorescence responses of (c) N,S-CDs and (d) N,Cl-CDs following exposure to DA and introduction of diverse interfering substances during a 120-min incubation, along with (e) photographs of the corresponding solutions under UV 365 nm light. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The fluorescence changes in (a) N,S-CDs and (b) N,Cl-CDs following the addition of dopamine (DA) at concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 μM, with an incubation period of 120 min. The linear relationships between fluorescence intensity and DA concentration are also presented for (c) N,S-CDs using a regression equation and (d) N,Cl-CDs utilizing the Stern–Volmer equation. (c) Photographs of the corresponding solutions after DA and Ag+ adding. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The comprehensive mechanism of dopamine (DA) detection and silver nanoparticle (AgNP) formation using (a) N,S-CDs and (b) N,Cl-CDs. Allowing N,Cl-CDs to react directly with Ag+ also causes fluorescent quenching. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Values obtained from XRD analysis to determine crystallite size and results from Raman spectra showing sp2 and sp3 correlations through ID/IG ratios and G-band shifting.

head.ce:title: Solution plasma synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from glucosamines: Comparative fluorescence modulation for dopamine detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The intriguing fluorescence characteristics of carbon dots (CDs) have led to many sensor applications, particularly for dopamine (DA), a molecule linked to various diseases. This study prepares CDs from glucose and glucosamine (nitrogen-free and nitrogen-containing precursors) using a simple solution plasma process (SPP). We explore how nitrogen elements and counterions in glucosamine (hydrochloride and sulfate) affect CD properties and their ability to modulate fluorescence for DA detection. Glucosamine offers three advantages over glucose: (i) single-step CD synthesis via SPP, (ii) enhanced fluorescence of CDs, and (iii) improved fluorescence response to DA. We investigate the DA detection capabilities of N,S-CDs (from glucosamine sulfate) and N,Cl-CDs (from glucosamine hydrochloride). Both can sense DA with distinct photoluminescence responses. N,S-CDs show selectivity and fluorescence brightening upon DA interaction, with a detection limit of 33.05 μM. N,Cl-CDs demonstrate higher sensitivity with a lower detection limit of 0.1212 μM through fluorescence quenching. Silver ions (Ag+) may also contribute to N,Cl-CDs quenching; however, the observed color change to gray due to silver nanoparticle (AgNP) formation helps pinpoint the quenching substance. The varied photoluminescence responses arise from different surface functional groups: N,S-CDs have amino-rich surfaces, while N,Cl-CDs have conjugated carbonyl-rich surfaces. This study illustrates the mechanisms of DA detection and AgNP formation, suggesting the potential of CDs in medical diagnostics as selective tools for disease diagnosis. Additionally, we compare the DA sensing methodology of our CDs with existing literature, highlighting the advantages of our sensor.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA), a neurotransmitter pivotal to physiological and cognitive functions, is fundamental in regulating mood, motivation, and motor control. Aberrations in DA levels have been associated with a spectrum of neurological and psychiatric disorders, including Tourette’s disorder [1], Parkinson’s disease [2], Alzheimer’s disease [3], etc. Therefore, the precise and sensitive detection of DA is paramount in clinical diagnostics, neuroscience research, and pharmaceutical development.

In recent years, the emergence of carbon dots (CDs) as versatile and biocompatible nanomaterials has revolutionized the landscape of sensing technologies [4]. CDs are the smallest carbon-based materials with unique fluorescence properties. They have gained prominence over traditional fluorescent materials for sensing due to their non-toxicity, biocompatibility, easy synthesis, excellent sensitivity, fast response, low cost, and versatility [5]. Their property relies on adjusting particle size [6], carbon core orderliness [7], and surface functional groups [8,9], which are valuable for broad sensing applications, including cell imaging [10,11], sensing for food contaminants [12,13], metal ions [14–17], pH [18,19], bacteria [20], and chemicals [21,22].

CDs can be synthesized through two primary approaches: (i) Top-down approach, entailing the breakdown of bulk carbon sources, such as carbon nanosheets, carbon fibers, carbon black, etc., through laser ablation, ultrasonication, electrochemical oxidation, etc.; and (ii) Bottom-up approach, involving the formation and assembly of carbon clusters, typically using carbon-containing substances like citric acid, glucose, glutathione, cysteine, etc. as starting materials. The bottom-up approach is generally favored for simplicity, allowing one-step synthesis with basic equipment setup. This approach also yields well-defined, monodispersed CDs with the desired functional groups by altering carbon precursors and synthesis conditions. Commonly employed bottom-up methods for preparing CDs include pyrolysis and hydrothermal methods, which yield CDs with high quantum efficiencies through relatively straightforward synthesis processes. However, both methods have their associated limitations: the high-temperature processing involved and the extended duration of the synthesis process, resulting in elevated energy consumption and long production times [23].

An alternative method, the “Solution Plasma Process (SPP)”, has recently been proposed to synthesize CDs. SPP represents a low-temperature plasma process that operates at atmospheric pressure. This technique has previously been employed to synthesize various nanomaterials, including metal and metal oxide nanoparticles [24–26], alloy clusters [27], and carbon materials [28]. The progression of SPP-generated carbon materials starts from activating molecules in the precursor solution with high applied energy, generating an abundance of reactive species. Subsequently, the resulting reactive molecules mobile and interact with other molecules in the solution, prompting them to enter an intermediate state. Finally, the intermediate molecules progress toward forming a carbon structure through either polymerization or carbonization reactions, depending on the starting materials [29]. In 2020, the SPP was initially documented for synthesizing nitrogen-doped CDs using a mixture of pyridine and water as precursors [30]. These CDs exhibited exceptional light emission characteristics, boasting an impressive quantum yield (QY) of 61%. The noteworthy aspect of this method lies in its ability to yield CDs within a mere 10-min timeframe under ambient room temperature and atmospheric pressure, effectively resolving the issue of excessive power and time consumption. Subsequently, in 2022, our research group introduced CD synthesis through the SPP from simple saccharides (i.e., fructose, glucose, and sucrose) to specify the SPP’s role in CD formation and elucidate how the molecular structure of these saccharides impacts the formation ability and characteristics of CDs [31]. The obtained CDs displayed an impressive maximum QY of 9.16%, notably high for saccharide-based CDs, particularly without heteroatom doping. They also exhibited an outstanding ability to detect Fe3+ ions. These two seminal publications underscore the SPP’s efficacy and highlight the influence of starting precursor on the resulting CDs’ characteristics and performance.

In the present study, our focus centers on the continuous investigation into how the chemical composition of precursor materials impacts the formation and attributes of CDs synthesized via the SPP. We choose nitrogen-containing glucose derivatives, specifically glucosamine, as primary precursors. It is well-established that heteroatom-doped CDs, notably nitrogen-doped CDs, exhibit remarkably enhanced fluorescence properties compared to their non-doped counterparts [32]. Our approach eliminates the need for additional doping agents and leverages the inherent nitrogen content of glucosamine to enhance CD fluorescence. Glucose is included as a reference for undoped samples. Additionally, we explore the effects of counterion variations by using two forms of glucosamine: glucosamine sulfate and glucosamine hydrochloride. The investigation provides new insights into how different counterions influence CD properties and synthesis outcomes. This research is among the first to systematically examine the impact of nitrogen-containing precursors on CD synthesis through SPP, contributing to the limited but growing body of literature on plasma-based CD formation. Table S1 summarizes four existing studies on liquid-phase plasma synthesis of CDs, highlighting key information from each. Additionally, we explore how varying surface chemistries of CDs influence DA detection, deepening our understanding of detection mechanisms and potentially paving the way for new sensor development.

By highlighting these aspects, we aim to advance the field of nanomaterials, particularly in DA detection and sustainable synthesis platforms. Our work addresses gaps in CD synthesis methods and opens new avenues for tailoring CD properties for specific applications, potentially impacting healthcare, neuroscience, and beyond.

Glucosamine hydrochloride (Glu-Cl, 98.0% purity) and glucosamine sulfate (Glu-S, 98.0% purity) were purchased from Acros Organics (Belgium) and the Government Pharmaceutical Organization (GPO) of Thailand, respectively. Glucose (98.0% purity), fructose (99.0% purity), sucrose (98.0% purity), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), and glycine hydrochloride (C2H5NO2·HCl, 97% purity) were purchased from Wako Pure Chemical Co., Inc. (Tokyo, Japan). Quinine sulfate dihydrate (QS, ≥99% purity), urea (CO(NH2)2, 99.5%), and sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98% purity) were supplied from Fluka (Germany). Sodium hydroxide pellet (NaOH, 99% purity) and hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%) were bought from ANaPURE® (Newzealand). Calcium chloride dihydrate (CaCl2·2H2O) and ferric chloride (FeCl3) were supplied from Riedel-deHaen (Germany). Silver nitrate (AgNO3) and magnesium nitrate hexahydrate (Mg(NO3)2·6H2O) were purchased from AnalaR (Belgium). Dopamine hydrochloride (C8H11NO2·HCl) was bought from Acros Chemical (Germany). Zinc chloride (ZnCl2) and ascorbic acid were obtained from QReC (New Zealand) and RCI Labscan (Thailand), respectively. Aniline and melamine were purchased from Aldrich® (Germany). Thiourea (CH4N2S, 99%) was brought from Loba Chemie (India). All chemical reagents and solvents were of analytical grades (except glucosamine sulfate, a pharmaceutical grade) and used without further purification. Type II water obtained from Gesellschaft Fuer Labortec™ (GFL 2012) water distillation apparatus was used throughout the experiments.

The stock solutions of glucose, glucosamine sulfate, and glucosamine hydrochloride were prepared by mixing 20 mM precursor solution and 0.1 mM NaOH to adjust the pH to 8. Then, 100 mL of the solution was transferred into a 100 mL glass beaker, which served as the plasma reactor. Within the reactor, 1 mm diameter tungsten electrodes surrounded by ceramic tubes and sealed at both ends with silicone plugs were inserted, as illustrated in Fig. 1 a. The tips of the electrodes were positioned at a separation distance of 0.5 mm. Subsequently, a bipolar electric discharge was initiated, operating with a pulse repetition frequency of 20 kHz and a pulse duration of 0.91 μs, sustaining plasma generation for 20 min.

Following the plasma treatment, the solution pH of all precursors lowered to 7, and the initially transparent solution became pale yellow. Notably, nitrogen-containing precursors (i.e., glucosamine sulfate and glucosamine hydrochloride) lead to the spontaneous generation of CDs, determined from the finding that the obtained products from these precursors exhibited a vibrant blue emission when exposed to UV 365 nm light without subsequent post-treatment procedures. Blue-emitting CDs offer key advantages for sensing applications due to their high photostability, maintaining fluorescence intensity over extended periods—crucial for reliable long-term measurements. They can be excited using widely available and cost-effective UV or blue light sources, facilitating seamless integration into existing detection systems. These features make blue-emitting CDs ideal for sensitive and accurate detection across diverse analytical setups. The increasing number of publications on blue CD emissions highlights their ongoing significance and potential in advancing sensing technologies [33–38].

Conversely, creating CDs from glucose (a nitrogen-free precursor) involved subjecting the plasma-treated samples to a post-heating process at 80 °C for 10 h. Subsequently, these samples were re-dispersed in distilled water to obtain CD solutions with a 10 mg · mL−1 concentration before demonstrating luminescence under UV light. Fig. 1b illustrates the chemical structures of glucose, glucosamine sulfate, and glucosamine hydrochloride, along with the observed phenomena described.

The CDs synthesized from glucose, glucosamine sulfate, and glucosamine hydrochloride were denoted as pristine CDs, N,S-CDs, and N,Cl-CDs, respectively. It is essential to mention that producing pristine CDs from glucose involved a two-step process that began with SPP followed by heat drying. On the contrary, only SPP was employed for one-step synthesis for nitrogen-doped CDs (N,S-CDs and N,Cl-CDs). Before further characterization, they were filtered through a 0.2 μm syringe filter (Whatman®).

The CDs’ fluorescence properties were assessed using a Horiba Scientific FluoroMax® 4 spectrofluorometer, employing a 2 nm slit width for both excitation and emission. The CDs’ absorption spectra were obtained with a Shimadzu UV–Visible 1800 spectrophotometer. An attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectrometer (Bruker Alpha, Platinum ATR) was employed to assess the surface functional groups of the different CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with Al Kɑ x-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos Model Axis Ultra DLD) was carried out to evaluate the chemical composition on the CDs’ surface. Raman spectroscopy (XploRA PLUS, Horiba Scientific) was conducted with a laser excitation wavelength of 532 nm, 1200-g⋅mm−1 grating, and 10% filter to characterize the CDs’ carbon molecular discipline. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, conducted with a Bruker D2 Phaser instrument, was employed to assess the structural orderliness of carbon. This analysis utilized Cu-Kɑ radiation with a wavelength of 0.154 nm. A nanoparticle analyzer (NanoPartica SZ-100V2 Series, Horiba Scientific) was employed to measure the hydrodynamic size and zeta potential. Additionally, the CDs’ morphology and size were examined using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM), specifically the Hitachi HT-7700 model, operated at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA through the CDs’ fluorescence behavior
ce:section.ce:para: Establishment of solution–based CD sensor: Given the known susceptibility of DA to oxidation in alkaline conditions, it was essential to assess the fluorescence stability of CDs across a pH range before proceeding with DA detection. The initial focus was, therefore, identifying the appropriate solution pH and incubation time to optimize the DA assay using CDs. The pH of CD solutions was adjusted to five different values, i.e., 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10. Then, the corresponding fluorescence emission responses were observed. The suitable incubation time was determined by mixing 0.1 mL of 100 μM DA with 1 mL of 1.0 mg · mL−1 CD solution. The mixture was then incubated at room temperature for 0, 5, 10, 15, 30, 45, 60, 90, 120, and 150 min before observing fluorescence spectra at excitation wavelengths of 380 and 400 nm for N,S-CDs and N,Cl-CDs, respectively. Subsequently, the specificity of CDs in detecting DA in the presence of various other chemicals, including glucose (Glu), fructose (Fru), sucrose (Suc), urea (Ure), ascorbic acid (AsA), glycine (Gly), aniline (Ani), melamine (Mel); and metal ions, including Ag+, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, and Fe3+ was examined. This evaluation involved adding 0.1 mL of a 100 μM interference solution into 1 mL of a 1.0 mg · mL−1 CD containing DA while maintaining control over the solution’s pH and adhering to a fixed incubation time, followed by fluorescence testing. Furthermore, the DA solution with various concentrations of 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 μM was mixed with the CD solution. Then, the fluorescence intensity was measured at a certain excitation wavelength, and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated.

Preparation of paper-based CD detector: Paper-based CDs were crafted by submerging Whatman® No.1 filter papers with a pore size of 90 mm in Petri dishes containing 20 mL of 10.0 mg · mL−1 of each CD solution. The soaked paper was subsequently extracted from the solution and allowed to air dry at room temperature for 48 h, ensuring it had completely dried. Then, 0.01 mL of a variety of chemicals (DA, Glu, Fru, Suc, Ure, AsA, Gly, Ani, and Mel) and metal ions (Ag+, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, and Fe3+) with a concentration of 1 mM were carefully dispensed onto the CDs-infused papers. These samples were left undisturbed at room temperature for 10 min before any light quenching or glowing under UV 365 nm light was observed using the naked eye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CDs (excitation energy dependent photoluminescence properties)
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of CDs were analyzed using UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. Results are shown in Fig. 2 a–c. All the synthesized CDs displayed a UV absorption peak at 272 nm, indicative of π–π∗ electronic transitions within the conjugated carbon core domain. Notably, the N,S-CDs and N,Cl-CDs exhibited an additional absorption shoulder at around 304 nm, attributed to n–π∗ transitions associated with the nitrogen-containing functional groups [39].

The photoluminescent characteristics of the CDs were explored in terms of excitation wavelengths and solution pH. The investigation encompassed a range of excitation wavelengths from 340 to 400 nm, using ten consecutive wavelengths and altering the solution pH across a span of 5 values, from 2 to 10, with increments of 2 pH units. While all CDs displayed excitation energy-dependent fluorescence properties, suggesting the presence of various surface states [40], the fluorescence intensity and stability exhibited differences. The maximum fluorescence intensity was observed with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm for pristine CDs and N,S-CDs, while the suitable excitation for N,Cl-CDs was 400 nm. The maximum fluorescence intensity emission of pristine CDs, N,S-CDs, and N,Cl-CDs were at 466, 434, and 451 nm, respectively.

The CDs’ absolute QY was determined using quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.10 M H2SO4 as a reference fluorescent compound with a known QY value (QYref) of 54%. The calculation was carried out using the following formula [41].
QYCDs = QYref × (mCDs / mref) × (nCDs / nref)2
In this equation, ‘m’ represents the slope of the linear relationship established by plotting the absorbance against integrated fluorescence intensity while varying the concentrations of CDs and quinine sulfate (0.10, 0.50, 1.00, 5.00, and 10.00 mM). The maxima concentration value deliberately maintained absorption below 0.10 at the specific excitation wavelength to mitigate potential inner filter effects and minimize the reabsorption effect [42]. Meanwhile, ‘n’ corresponds to the refractive index of the solvent. The subscription ‘ref’ and ‘CDs’ are represented for the reference fluorescence compound and the synthesized CDs, respectively.

Fig. 2d illustrates the QY percentages (QY%) with the QY values arranged in descending order: N,Cl-CDs at 22.51%, N,S-CDs at 15.37%, and pristine CDs at 6.19%. These findings show that adding a single nitrogen substitution to the glucose molecule (as in glucosamine) simplifies the synthesis process to a one-step SPP. This approach reduces the processing time to only 20 min and significantly improves the photoluminescent properties of CDs, resulting in QY values that are more than twice as high as those of pristine CDs.

Compared to CDs synthesized from glucosamine using other methods (see Table S2), the resulting CDs in this study may exhibit a lower QY. However, our synthesis was conducted without doping agents. Although NaOH was added to adjust the pH, the final product had a pH of 7 after plasma treatment. We prioritized an environmentally friendly approach, which may result in lower QYs but offers sustainability benefits. Despite the reduced QY, our CDs demonstrate sufficient fluorescence for practical DA sensing applications. We believe this method’s environmental advantages justify the QY trade-off.

The CDs’ chemical structure and morphology were characterized through TEM, XRD, Raman, FTIR, XPS, and solid UV–visible spectroscopy to complete their structural model. TEM images of all CDs are depicted in Fig. 3 a to assess the CDs’ morphology and lattice fringe. The observations revealed that N,S-CDs exhibited carbon particle aggregation with an average size of 4 nm. However, both pristine CDs and N,Cl-CDs displayed larger particle sizes of approximately 10 and 13 nm with distinct carbon lattice fringes.

The measurement of interplanar distances within the lattice fringe was consistent with the CDs’ XRD pattern. The lattice spacings, approximately 0.22 nm, corresponded to the (001) plane, while interplanar spacings of 0.32 nm indicated the (200) facet of graphitic carbon [43]. These observations are presented in Fig. 3b, with two diffraction peaks located around 24° and 40° found in pristine CDs and N,Cl-CDs. However, N,S-CDs exhibited a broadened diffraction peak at around 22°.

The carbon hybridization alignment and order were investigated using Raman spectroscopy, with sp3 disorder indicative of amorphous carbon and sp2 crystallinity associated with graphitic carbon, as illustrated in Fig. 3c. The corresponding values from XRD and Raman spectra results are also shown in Table 1 . The focus parameters are crystallite size, the intensity ratios of the D band to the G band (ID/IG), and the wavenumber of the G-band. It is important to note that the crystallite sizes reported in Table 1, which were determined from XRD measurements, reflect the average size of the carbon lattice. In contrast, the particle sizes obtained from TEM measurements encompass the CDs’ carbon lattice and surface features. As a result, the crystallite sizes are generally smaller than the particle sizes observed via TEM. Despite this difference in measurement techniques, both methods reveal a consistent size trend: N,Cl-CDs > pristine CDs ≫ N,S-CDs. A more extended Raman shift of the G band in N,S-CDs and N,Cl-CDs suggested increased nitrogen content in the carbon core [44]. The elevated ID/IG values observed in pristine CDs, N,S-CDs, and N,Cl-CDs could also be attributed to the incorporation and higher nitrogen content.

The surface characteristics, including functional groups and elemental compositions, were assessed using FTIR and XPS. In the FTIR spectra (Fig. 3d), we observed common functional groups in all CDs, including C–H and O–H stretching, C=O stretching, C=C bending, and C–O stretching. Distinctive functional groups identified on the surface of N,S-CDs were N–H primary amine stretching at 3561 cm−1 and S=O stretching at 1118 cm−1, while that of N,Cl-CDs were C≡N stretching at 2350 cm−1, C=O conjugated acid stretching at 1765 cm−1, and C–Cl bending at 778 cm−1. Notably, the peak of N–H primary amine was outstanding in N,S-CDs, signifying the abundance of primary amine (amino) groups (–NH2) on their surface. On the other hand, the surface of N,Cl-CDs exhibited a prevalence of conjugated carbonyl groups (=C–(C=O)–). However, no specific functional groups were particularly abundant on the surface of pristine CDs.

XPS analysis was conducted to determine the atomic composition and delineate the proportion of each element on the CDs’ surface through curve fitting and peak integration. Fig. 3e displays a full scan of XPS survey spectra and elemental percentages. Two prominent peaks, corresponding to C 1s and O 1s, were identified in all CDs. While both nitrogen-doped CDs featured the N 1s peak, N,S-CDs exhibited an additional peak corresponding to S 2p and N,Cl-CDs revealed the presence of peaks related to Cl 1s and Cl 2p. Additionally, the observed remarkably higher nitrogen content in N,S-CDs (10.77%) over N,Cl-CDs (2.83%) confirms amino-rich functional groups on the N,S-CDs’ surface.

High-resolution XPS was utilized to characterize the elemental bonding in greater detail. The C 1s spectra of all CDs (depicted in Fig. 4 a, b, and c) were deconvoluted and pronounced three similar peaks: (i) at 283.70 eV, corresponding to C–C and C=C bonds; (ii) at 285.30 eV, indicative of C–O bonds; and (iii) at 286.50 eV, linked to C=O bonds. Additional peaks of HO–C=O bonds at 288.27 eV and C–N bonds at 284.40 eV were found in pristine CDs and N,S-CDs, respectively, while N,Cl-CDs revealed both augmentative peaks. These additional peaks align with the findings from FTIR results, highlighting the existence of carbonyl groups in pristine CDs, C≡N groups in N,S-CDs, and conjugated carbonyl groups in N,Cl-CDs. In the O 1s spectra of all CDs (Fig. 4d, e, and f), various peaks related to C–O, C=O (except in N,S-CDs), C–O–C, and C–OH were identified at around 531.10 eV, 531.93 eV, and 533.04 eV, respectively. The high-resolution N 1s spectra (Fig. 4g and h) revealed the presence of three distinct peaks: the C–N–C bond at 398.42 eV, the N–(C)3 bond at 399.35 eV, and the N–H bond at 400.05 eV. Notably, the first peak was only observed in the N,Cl-CDs. The results clarify the insertion of counter ions (S and Cl) on the surface of N,S-CDs and N,Cl-CDs are presented in Fig. 4i. In the high-resolution S 2p spectrum, two discernible peaks were observed at 167.34 eV for S 2p3/2 and 168.25 eV for S 2p1/2. The Cl 2p spectrum also displayed two peaks at 197.93 eV for Cl 2p3/2 and 199.51 eV for Cl 2p1/2.

Characterizations mentioned above suggest that the QY order observed in this study (N,Cl-CDs > N,S-CDs > Pristine CDs) is linked to two essential factors: (i) nitrogen incorporation and (ii) unique surface states. (i) Incorporating nitrogen into the carbon skeleton, mainly through glucosamine, enhances photoluminescent properties by explicitly introducing additional energy levels related to graphitic nitrogen and nitrogen-related surface states. This leads to more emission energy trap states, resulting in increased radiative recombinations of electron-hole pairs, increasing the QY. Consequently, nitrogen-doped CDs (N,S-CDs and N,Cl-CDs) exhibit more vigorous fluorescence intensity than undoped CDs (pristine CDs). (ii) Oxygen-related surface functional groups, especially in N,Cl-CDs, create additional trap states within the structure, contributing to defect state emission. This also results in higher QYs due to enhanced photoluminescence.

The optical band gap of CDs was determined by converting UV–visible data into absorption coefficient (α). Then, the energy levels were calculated by extrapolating a linear fit through Tauc plot analysis using the following equation [45].
αհν = A(հν–Eg)1/n
Where ‘հ’ is Plank’s constant, ‘ν’ is the frequency of light, and ‘n’ is the natural electron transition. The value of ‘n’ can be either 1/2 or 2, depending on the inherent characteristics of an indirect or direct allowed transition. Via direct calculation, the optical band gaps of pristine CDs, N,S-CDs, and N,Cl-CDs were estimated to be 3.09, 3.00, and 2.94 eV, respectively (Fig. 5 ).

Generally, the disparity of the CDs’ band gap energies can be attributed to three principal factors: CD size, core structure, and surface state [46]. The XRD and TEM analyses revealed that despite pristine CDs and N,Cl-CDs having similar crystal and particle sizes, their band gap energies differed, suggesting no direct relationship between band gap energy and CD size. In other words, the core structure and the surface state were two primary factors contributing to this study’s band gap energy change. Concerning the core structure, the ID/IG ratios followed this sequence: N,Cl-CDs > N,S-CDs > pristine CDs, indicated a greater prevalence of defects in the carbon core of N,Cl-CDs over N,S-CDs, and pristine CDs. This increased prevalence of defects was attributed to nitrogen insertion, resulting in an additional conduction band with a lower energy level. Fig. 6 illustrates the relationship between the characteristics of CDs and their energy band gaps.

Considering the surface state, the two primary functional groups with notable effects were nitrogen-related and conjugated carbonyl groups. N,Cl-CDs exhibited the lowest energy band gap due to both carbonyl and nitrogen-related functional groups on their surface. In contrast, N,S-CDs featured only a single dominant surface state, i.e., nitrogen-related groups, while pristine CDs lacked specific abundant functional groups. Consequently, the CDs’ energy band gaps followed this sequence: pristine CDs > N,S-CDs > N,Cl-CDs. This finding emphasizes the significant impact of surface functional groups, especially carbonyl and amine groups, on the electronic properties of CDs, with the former having a more pronounced effect in reducing band gap.

The utilization of CDs for DA sensing was categorized into two approaches: (i) liquid form, i.e., solution-based CD sensor, and (ii) solid form, i.e., paper-based CD detector. Notably, the evaluation of DA detection performance was limited to N,S-CDs and N,Cl-CDs. These choices stemmed from the fact that pristine CDs did not exhibit any alteration in their fluorescence emission upon interaction with DA. The lack of clearly abundant functional groups on their surface could be the reason for this phenomenon.

Solution–based CD sensor: The fluorescence emission stability of CDs under varying pH conditions is depicted in Fig. 7 a. Notably, N,Cl-CDs displayed consistent fluorescence intensity between pH 4 and 10, while N,S-CDs exhibited a narrower range of fluorescence stability, specifically from pH 6 to 8. Consequently, the pH of CD solutions was adjusted to 6, a level that not only stabilized the fluorescence intensity but also prevented the self-polymerization of DA, which occurs when the solution’s pH exceeds 7.4 [47].

The CDs’ sensitivity in detecting DA was assessed by monitoring their fluorescence emission behaviors (Fig. 7b). Interestingly, both N,S-CDs and N,Cl-CDs exhibited noticeable changes in fluorescence intensity after a 120-min incubation period. However, the fluorescence responses differed: N,S-CDs showed a slight fluorescence increase, while N,Cl-CDs displayed a gradual decrease in fluorescence (quenching). Then, the selectivity of CDs for DA detection was examined by introducing interfering molecules into the CDs-DA system (Fig. 7c and d). The fluorescence emission intensity was measured after the incubation was completed. It was observed that N,S-CDs exhibited high selectivity for DA detection, with no significant change in fluorescence intensity after adding all interferences. However, the presence of Ag+ notably affected N,Cl-CDs, resulting in a dramatic fluorescence quenching effect. Photographs of the corresponding solutions under UV 365 nm light are also depicted in Fig. 7e. To compare the affinity of N,Cl-CDs for DA and Ag+, the order of adding these chemical molecules in equal amounts and concentrations was reversed and compared. Results are shown in Fig. S1. Notably, adding Ag+ to the N,Cl-CDs solution resulted in more pronounced fluorescence quenching than DA.

Moreover, the sensitivity of CDs to DA was assessed by subjecting them to varying DA concentrations and tracking changes in photoluminescence behavior (Fig. 8 a and b). Given the distinct fluorescence responses upon interaction with DA, the LOD was determined using different calculation methods: linear regression analysis [48] of the degree of FL intensity enhancement versus DA concentration for N,S-CDs (Fig. 8c), and the Stern–Volmer equation [49] of the fluorescence intensity ratio versus DA concentration for N,Cl-CDs (Fig. 8d), as described below.
Regressive equation: FL–FL0 = KSV × CDA
Stern–Volmer equation: (FL/FL0) = 1 + (KSV × CDA)
LOD = 3 × SD

Here, FL0 and FL denote the highest fluorescence intensity at a specific excitation wavelength for CDs before and after the addition of DA for 120 min, respectively. KSV represents the Stern–Volmer constant, which reflects the affinity between CDs and DA. CDA is the concentration of DA, whereas SD is the standard deviation. Besides, the LOD was calculated to be 33.05 μM, while N,Cl-CDs exhibited a lower LOD of 0.1212 μM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (see Table 2 ). Since various types of CDs from existing literature have been studied for DA detection, we have compiled their synthesis methods and performance as DA probes in Table S3 for comparison.

Furthermore, the reproducibility of the synthesized N,Cl-CDs and N,S-CDs as sensors was evaluated under various conditions. For DA detection, we tested the response of N,S-CDs and N,Cl-CDs to ten different chemicals (DA, Glu, Fru, Suc, Ure, AsA, Gly, Ani, Mel, and Thiourea) and seven metal ions (Ag+, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, and Fe3+). However, the results indicated that the reproducibility for DA detection was not robust across all tested conditions.

In contrast, the N,Cl-CDs exhibited excellent reproducibility as a sensor for Ag+. This was demonstrated by an off/on fluorescence emission response: the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly upon the addition of Ag+ (100 μM, 0.1 mL) to the N,Cl-CDs solution, and subsequently, the fluorescence brightened again when thiourea (100 μM, 0.1 mL) was added, as illustrated in the Supporting Video. The thiourea addition enhanced the fluorescence intensity by forming a complex with Ag+, freeing the N,Cl-CDs that Ag+ had capped [50]. These results indicate that while N,Cl-CDs are highly effective for Ag+ sensing, their reproducibility for DA detection is limited.

Paper-based CD detector: The paper-based CDs for detecting various chemicals and ions were prepared as an economical and facile solid sensor using ordinary cellulose filter paper soaked in a CD solution. As depicted in Fig. S2, N,S-CDs emitted a blue light, while N,Cl-CDs emitted a lighter blue color when observed under UV 365 nm light. Unfortunately, no distinct outcomes were observed with any tested molecules for N,S-CDs, implying that molecular mobility of the N,S-CDs and DA are pivotal for allowing surface-induced reactions. On the other hand, a change in emission color occurred for N,Cl-CDs, shifting from light blue to light green after interacting with DA and Ag+. However, the change was not as noticeable as in the case of liquid-based sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential mechanisms of DA detection and AgNP formation using N,S-CDs and N,Cl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Mechanisms behind the sensitivity and selectivity of CDs, which lead to fluorescence brightening in N,S-CDs, and fluorescence quenching in N,Cl-CDs during DA detection, were investigated through zeta-potential measurement, dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis, and UV–visible spectrophotometry. These investigations revealed changes in overall charge, alterations in hydrodynamic diameter, and the formation of molecular/particle complexes, respectively. Results are shown in Fig. 9 , along with the corresponding comprehensive mechanisms shown in Fig. 10 .

Fig. 9a illustrates the zeta-potential values and hydrodynamic diameters of N,S-CDs, and N,Cl-CDs and the changes in these features upon interacting with DA and Ag+. The N,S-CDs and N,Cl-CDs displayed zeta potential values of −48.47 mV and −62.72 mV, signifying negative surface charges attributed to the amino-rich and conjugated acid carbonyl-rich surfaces, respectively. Besides, more negative values than 40 mV indicated their excellent electrostatic stability and effective nanoparticle dispersion [51].

After introducing DA into the N,S-CDs solution, a remarkable shift of overall surface charge from negative to positive occurred. This transformation is supposed to be a consequence of the polymerization of DA to polydopamine (PDA), substantiated by the emergence of a novel absorption peak within the 480–600 nm range (Fig. 9b), indicating pi-pi stacking interactions between CDs and PDA [48]. As illustrated in Fig. 10a, we supposed that DA molecules can bind to amino and hydroxyl groups on the CDs’ surface through H-bonding and other interactions before undergoing auto-oxidation. During this process, DA is oxidized in the presence of oxygen to form reactive o-quinone intermediates. Amino groups on the CDs’ surface may play a role in facilitating this oxidation (see Fig. S3). The reactive o-quinone intermediates generated in the oxidation step can undergo further reactions, including nucleophilic attack by the remaining amino groups on DA molecules, leading to PDA coating. Two additional confirmatory observations indicating the presence of PDA on the surface of N,S-CDs are (i) the considerable increase in hydrodynamic diameter of the CDs−DA and (ii) the shift in zeta potential from negative to positive. Moreover, it is hypothesized that the PDA coating contributes to fluorescence enhancement.

Upon introducing DA into the N,Cl-CDs solution, no change in surface charge (which remained negative) or hydrodynamic size was observed for the CDs−DA complex. Additionally, there was no new absorption peak within the 480–600 nm range. The primary change was an increase in zeta potential from −62.72 mV to −35.97 mV, likely due to the adsorption of DA molecules on the CDs’ surface through hydrogen bonding with carboxylic groups and other interactions. This interaction facilitates electron transfer between the amino groups of DA and the carbonyl groups of CDs, leading to fluorescence quenching [52]. Moreover, the quenching effect observed when Ag+ was added to N,Cl-CDs may be attributed to residual hydroxyl groups on the CDs’ surface and DA molecules. These hydroxyl groups can donate electrons to reduce silver ions (Ag+) to silver atoms (Ag0), eventually forming silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) that immobilize on the CDs’ surface (Fig. 10b). The formation of AgNPs is evidenced by a new absorption peak at 410 nm and a noticeable color change in the solution from light yellow to gray (Fig. 9c). Further validation of AgNP formation is supported by (i) a significant increase in the hydrodynamic diameter of the CDs–DA–Ag complex (indicating the formation of the CDs–DA–AgNP composite) and (ii) a transition in zeta potential from negative to positive.

In summary, this study demonstrates the effective use of nitrogen-doped CDs synthesized via SPP for DA sensing, with N,S-CDs and N,Cl-CDs each exhibiting distinct photoluminescence responses. N,S-CDs provide high selectivity for DA detection through fluorescence enhancement, while N,Cl-CDs show superior sensitivity with a lower LOD achieved via fluorescence quenching. Additionally, N,Cl-CDs are responsive to Ag+, resulting in a noticeable color change due to AgNPs forming, further distinguishing the quenching mechanisms. However, the effectiveness of these CDs is limited to liquid-based systems, as paper-based formats did not yield clear or distinguishable results. This work underscores the potential of SPP-derived nitrogen-doped CDs as versatile, selective, and sensitive tools for liquid-phase DA detection, with additional utility in detecting Ag+.

We successfully synthesized nitrogen-doped CDs using the SPP with glucosamines as carbon precursors, providing a simple, one-step method that enhances fluorescence properties compared to glucose-derived CDs. The primary innovation lies in the selective functionalization of CDs, where N,S-CDs and N,Cl-CDs feature amino-rich and conjugated carbonyl-rich surfaces, respectively. These surface characteristics enable differential DA detection, with N,S-CDs exhibiting fluorescence brightening and N,Cl-CDs showing fluorescence quenching. Notably, N,Cl-CDs demonstrated superior sensitivity with a lower LOD (0.1212 μM), whereas N,S-CDs offered better selectivity. Additionally, N,Cl-CDs were also responsive to Ag+, with a distinct color change due to AgNP formation, providing an additional mechanism for quenching differentiation. This study highlights the potential of SPP-derived nitrogen-doped CDs as selective and sensitive tools for DA detection in liquid-based systems.
